nativeRange.habitat: Generally found among coral and coral rubble in lagoons and reef flats, typically from the shallows down to around 20 metres depth. Tends to shelter in crevices and beneath ledges by day and forage over the reef at twilight.

decorPreferences: live rock with abundant caves and overhangs
mature reef tank with stable parameters
subdued lighting in at least part of the tank

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied carnivore-leaning diet: frozen mysis and brine shrimp, small pieces of fish or shellfish, sinking pellets, and occasional algae wafers. Wild individuals are reported to feed on detritus, algae, and small invertebrates. Best fed in the evening or after lights-out, when it is most active.

goodWith: peaceful reef fish that won't harass shrimp
most LPS and SPS corals (with monitoring)
snails and hermit crabs of similar or larger size
avoidWith: zoanthids and palythoa colonies (commonly picked at)
feather duster worms and tubeworms (commonly eaten)
small ornamental shrimp and very small inverts (predatory risk)
lionfish, large hawkfish, large wrasses, triggerfish, puffers, eels
Coral Banded Shrimp and Arrow Crabs (territorial conflict)
conspecifics other than a single mated pair (territorial)

summary: The Marbled Saron Shrimp is a nocturnal Indo-Pacific shrimp that hides in reef crevices by day and forages after dark. It is generally considered compatible with reef tanks only with caution, as individuals have been reported to pick at zoanthids, tubeworms, and small ornamental shrimp.
careNotes: The Marbled Saron Shrimp is most active at dusk and through the night, and is widely distributed across the Indo-Pacific, from the Red Sea and East Africa eastward to Hawaii. One of its most distinctive traits is a color change between day and night: individuals display a marbled brown-and-green pattern during the day that shifts to a predominantly red coloration after dark, which is reported to aid camouflage in twilight and low-light conditions. Males can be distinguished from females by tufts of bristly cirri (hair-like projections) along the carapace and by noticeably elongated first walking legs. Reef compatibility is debated in the hobby: opinion ranges from 'not reef safe' to 'with caution', and the general consensus is that established individuals tend to pick at zoanthids, palythoa, soft corals, tubeworms, and feather dusters, and are predatory toward smaller ornamental shrimp and other small invertebrates. Most hobbyists treat it as a shrimp best suited to a fish-only-with-live-rock system or a reef tank where those vulnerable species are absent. Standard reef parameters apply (24–26°C, pH 8.0–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12), along with adequate calcium, magnesium, iodine, and trace elements to support proper molting. Like all shrimp, it is highly intolerant of copper-based medications, which should never be used in a tank where it is kept. On introduction the species tends to be shy and skittish; it may not emerge from hiding for several days. Provide abundant caves and overhangs in mature live rock to give it a sense of security. Best kept singly or as a mated pair, as conspecifics generally fight.
breedingNotes: The Marbled Saron Shrimp is generally considered very difficult to breed in captivity, and the trade supply is essentially entirely wild-caught. Larvae pass through multiple zoea and postlarval stages that require stable salinity (commonly cited around 35–37 ppt) and clean larval-rearing conditions that are not typically achievable in a display aquarium. Adults can be sexed by the bristly cirri tufts and elongated first walking legs of males. Pairs may be maintained together, but successful larval rearing has not been widely reported by hobbyists.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters (72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.023-1.025), care level, family (Hippolytidae), diet (carnivore), supplements (calcium, magnesium, iodine, trace elements), origin (Hawaii, Indonesia, Maldives), copper intolerance, and reef-safety flag (LiveAquaria rates this species as not reef safe).

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics — Saron Shrimp, Marble Shrimp
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (~2 in), tank size (24 gal minimum), temperament (peaceful), diet (carnivore), origin (Indo-Pacific, Hawaii), and reef compatibility ('with caution').

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder — Saron Shrimp Detailed Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (~40 L / 10 gal per individual + 5 gal per additional), adult size (up to 8 cm / 3 in), water parameters (22-26°C, pH 8.0-8.4, dKH 8-12, calcium 400-450 ppm), lifespan (up to 3 years), and reef safety (will eat zoanthids, montipora, pocillopora, bubble-tip anemone polyps). Source also lists unsuitable tankmates and confirms wild-caught only / no documented captive breeding.

additional.name: Reeflings — Marbled Shrimp (Saron marmoratus)
additional.notes: Third secondary used to average size (~6 cm) and tank size (80 L / 21 gal), and to confirm temperament as semi-aggressive / territorial toward conspecifics. Cross-checked salinity (SG 1.023-1.025) and copper sensitivity.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Saron marmoratus
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Hippolytidae, Decapoda), authorship (Olivier, 1811), Indo-Pacific distribution from the Red Sea to Hawaii, lagoon/coral-rubble habitat, depth range (typically not below ~12 m), and nocturnal camouflage-based behavior.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Sources do not give a typical molt interval. Adequate iodine and trace elements are commonly cited as important for proper molting; molts are generally left in the tank to be consumed for calcium reuptake.
crustacean.breedingNotes: No reliably reported captive-breeding success. Larval stages require stable, clean rearing conditions outside of a typical display tank. The trade is essentially wild-caught.
